items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What does the Buddhist doctrine of anatta teach?
items.correct: There is no permanent, unchanging self or soul — what we call 'self' is a constantly changing flow of five aggregates (skandhas). This is distinctively Buddhist and differs from the Hindu atman.
items.misconception: Buddhists believe in a soul like the Christian or Hindu version.
items.random_wrong: Anatta means Buddhists have no personality.
items.why: Anatta is central to Buddhism and explicitly rejects the Hindu atman doctrine. Students assume all Eastern religions share the same soul concept, missing the deliberate contrast Buddhism draws.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What does the Golden Rule say?
items.correct: Treat others as you'd like to be treated
items.random_wrong: Money is the root of evil
items.why: It's Treat others as you'd like to be treated. “Always tell the truth” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is closest to empathy?
items.why: It's Understanding others' feelings. “Earning lots of money” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many major religions teach a version of the Golden Rule?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
